questions.question: The charge on a single electron is:
questions.explanation: Electrons carry a negative elementary charge of $-1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$.

questions.question: The SI unit of electric charge is the:
questions.explanation: Charge is measured in coulombs (C). One coulomb is the charge of approximately $6.25 \times 10^{18}$ electrons.

questions.question: Two objects with the same type of charge will:
options: Have no effect on each other
questions.explanation: Like charges repel. Unlike charges attract. This is a fundamental law of electrostatics.

questions.question: A positively charged rod is brought near a negatively charged object. They will:
options: Have no effect on each other
questions.explanation: Unlike charges attract. The positive rod and negative object will pull toward each other.

questions.question: Which of the following is a good electrical conductor?
questions.explanation: Copper is a metal with free electrons that can move easily, making it an excellent conductor.

questions.question: Which of the following is a good electrical insulator?
questions.explanation: Rubber has tightly bound electrons that cannot move freely, making it a good insulator.

questions.question: When a plastic rod is rubbed with a wool cloth, the rod becomes negatively charged. This is because:
options: Protons move from the wool to the rod
Electrons move from the wool to the rod
Protons move from the rod to the wool
Neutrons are created in the rod
questions.explanation: Only electrons are transferred during charging by friction. Protons remain fixed in the nucleus.

questions.question: The law of conservation of charge states that: (Select all that apply)
options: Charge can be created but not destroyed
Charge can be destroyed but not created
Charge can be neither created nor destroyed
Charge can be transferred between objects
Charge is always increasing in the universe
questions.explanation: Charge cannot be created or destroyed — it is conserved. However, charge can be transferred between objects (e.g., by friction or conduction). Both C and D are true statements of the law.

questions.question: A negatively charged rod is brought near a neutral metal sphere without touching it. The side of the sphere closest to the rod becomes:
questions.explanation: Electrons in the sphere are repelled to the far side, leaving positive charge on the near side. This is charging by induction.

questions.question: In an atom, protons are located in the:
questions.explanation: Protons and neutrons form the nucleus at the center of the atom. Electrons orbit in shells around it.

questions.question: A proton carries a charge that is:
options: Equal and opposite to that of an electron
Equal and same as that of an electron
Twice the charge of an electron
questions.explanation: A proton has charge $+1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$; an electron has $-1.6 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$. They are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign.

questions.question: When an object becomes charged, the particle that is most commonly transferred is the:
questions.explanation: Electrons are small, light, and loosely bound to atoms. Protons are fixed in the nucleus and cannot move between objects.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered the fundamentals of electric charge!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review charging by induction and the difference between conductors and insulators.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on which particles move during charging and the conservation of charge.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: positive and negative charges, coulomb as the unit of charge, and conductors vs. insulators.
